era_context: The early Iron Age I in the Levant and Eastern Mediterranean; the Late Bronze Age collapse has occurred within the past two centuries. Egypt under the late Twentieth Dynasty (Ramesses IX–XI); the New Kingdom is in terminal decline. In Mesopotamia, the Middle Assyrian period is ending; Neo-Assyrian expansion has not yet begun. Greece is in the Early Iron Age (Protogeometric period); the Mycenaean palatial system has collapsed. In South Asia, the Vedic period is well established in the Indo-Gangetic plain; the Rigveda may have been composed or was in oral transmission during this era.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this specific year)
East Asia (Chinese sources are sparse for this period; Shang dynasty had ended ~1046 BCE, early Zhou period records are fragmentary)
Mesoamerica (no written records; archaeological dating is approximate)

events.title: Reign of Ramesses XI in Egypt (approximate period)
events.description: Ramesses XI (c. 1099–1069 BCE) was the last pharaoh of the Twentieth Dynasty. By this point in his reign, Egypt's territorial power had contracted significantly, and the High Priests of Amun at Thebes wielded increasing autonomous authority. The division of Egypt into a northern (Tanite) and southern (Theban) sphere was becoming institutionalized. This period marks the effective end of the New Kingdom and the beginning of the Third Intermediate Period.

sources.name: Karnak temple inscriptions and Theban tomb records

events.certainty_note: Ramesses XI's reign is dated c. 1099–1069 BCE by most Egyptologists, placing 1006 BCE within his rule. However, absolute dating of this period relies on astronomical calculations and king-list synchronisms, introducing ±5–10 year uncertainty.

events.title: Vedic period consolidation in the Indo-Gangetic plain
events.description: During the early Iron Age in South Asia, Vedic Aryan societies were establishing agricultural settlements and pastoral economies across the Indo-Gangetic plain. The Rigveda, composed orally during this broad era (c. 1500–1200 BCE or later, depending on scholarly dating), reflects the religious and social structures of these communities. By 1006 BCE, the transition from the Rigvedic to the Yajurvedic period may have been underway, with increasing emphasis on ritual and sacrifice.

sources.name: Rigveda and Yajurveda (oral tradition, later compiled)
Archaeological evidence from sites like Hastinapur and Painted Grey Ware cultures

events.certainty_note: The dating of Vedic texts is highly contested. Traditional Hindu chronology places the Rigveda much earlier (c. 1500 BCE or earlier); Western scholarship often dates it to 1200–1000 BCE or later. Archaeological evidence (Painted Grey Ware, iron tools) suggests iron-working was becoming established in this period, consistent with the early Iron Age.

disconfirming_evidence: The absolute chronology of the late Twentieth Dynasty in Egypt remains debated. Some scholars argue for a 'low chronology' that would shift these dates by 50–100 years. The dating of Vedic texts is particularly contentious: some scholars place the Rigveda as early as 1500 BCE, others as late as 800 BCE, making any specific assignment to 1006 BCE speculative. No major events are definitively attested to this specific year in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: This year falls in a period of sparse written documentation outside Egypt. Egyptian sources (temple inscriptions, administrative records) are relatively abundant but focus on elite activities and religious matters. The Vedic tradition was oral and was not committed to writing until much later (possibly 500 BCE or after), so dating specific events to this year is impossible. Archaeological evidence (pottery, settlement patterns, metallurgy) provides indirect evidence of cultural development but cannot pinpoint events to a single year. The historical record is heavily skewed toward Egypt and the Mediterranean; South Asian, Mesopotamian, and other regions are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Ramesses XI's reign spans c. 1099–1069 BCE; 1006 BCE falls within this period
